Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit jhinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington’s new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop.

Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lower- quality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation detracted from results, as our diversified approach was out of step with the aggressive rally. The real assets portfolio was the primary detractor due to negative returns for energy stocks and U.S. real estate investment trusts. An overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms — also weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. On the positive side, the fund benefited from having an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. Allocation in the fixed-income portfolio was a small contributor, primarily as a result of an overweight in emerging-market bonds.

Underlying manager performance detracted somewhat, with nearly all of the shortfall occurring in the equity portfolio.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors’ rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don’t attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Strong Fundraising, Strategic Acquisitions, And Solid Financials Support A Bullish Outlook
data.description:
Thesis

Blue Owl (OWL) is a very interesting investment option that mainly interests investors who want quality proposals with a medium- to long-term focus.

The stock’s course so far, it was listed in December 2020, shows that there is genuine buying interest, with shareholders already gaining +110% total return in less than 5 years, including the dividend, which is at 4.4%.

It has completed a cycle of targeted acquisitions, real estate credit, digital infrastructure, asset backed finance, insurance, which have already been integrated and bring synergies in capital inflows and origination.

In the second quarter of 2025, it recorded the 17th consecutive increase in management fees, +32% annually, and a record raising of capital, $12 billion.

The diversification of revenues in sectors of high demand, such as AI driven data centers, alternative credit and net lease, gives stability and growth prospects.

With a target of FRE > $3 billion and management fees > $5 billion, and with geographic expansion in EMEA and APAC, the stock shows real upside potential over 2 to 3 years for investors who seek exposure to thematic alternative assets with strong execution discipline.

Stock Overview

The stock follows a long term sideways upward pattern, proving that it suits more investors who seek positions and not short-term trades.

Besides, because its volatility seems quite high, having in fact a beta of 1.7, the interested party should expect several ups and downs in daily activity.

However, the stock gave a buy signal at $15, when on the occasion of Trump’s “Liberation Day” it was hit sideways and respected the support of the above-mentioned trend.

If we accept the existing formation, which for now is in effect, we see that a possible target is the zone of $29, +45%.

At the same time, the stock gives a good dividend of about 4.4% ($0.9, quart. distributed), which adds to the already good return of the stock. This, in fact, keeps increasing, from $0.09 at the end of 2021, reaching $0.23 per quarter, proving on one hand the good course of the company, and on the other hand the willingness of the management to keep shareholders satisfied. This is something that particularly interests potential investors, also noting that for now, the management appears especially capable, as we will see below.

Blue Owl on a growth path, the acquisitions that change the game

Blue Owl Capital entered the second quarter of 2025 with momentum and a goal to confirm that it is not just another alternative investment manager, but an organization that knows how to turn strategic moves into measurable results. The numbers it announced show that the company is not only maintaining its growth, but strengthening it, and a large part of this success is due to a series of well targeted acquisitions.

The period from 2024 to 2025 saw four key developments, including the purchase of a real estate credit platform that dominated single asset CMBS, the expansion into digital infrastructure through hyperscale data center expertise, the combination of a top asset backed finance team from Adelie, and the establishment of an insurance unit to link all operations with stable long-term capital.

What stands out here is the way Blue Owl executed the acquisitions. It does not chase size for the sake of size, it targets sectors where it can gain a leading position and fully utilize the existing distribution and origination platform. The result is that the new activities did not remain stagnant after the purchase, but started delivering almost immediately. From my point of view, I consider that this drastically reduces the execution risk that usually accompanies large M&A moves.

The proof of the success of these acquisitions is already visible. Blue Owl has gathered $3.5 billion in capital from strategies that did not exist two years ago, with the top example being BOSE, Blue Owl Strategic Equity, GP led secondaries. BOSE invests in continuation funds, offering liquidity to LPs and extending the duration of the best assets. It is a market in its early steps, but with huge growth potential, and OWL shows determined to lead.

At the same time, in the alternative credit sector, the partnerships with LendingClub and Capital on Tap secure quality loans with spreads over 200 bps compared to similar public securities. The connection with the insurance channel allows the channelling of these returns to clients who seek stable, secured assets. This, in my opinion, is a double gain, it stabilizes revenue flows and strengthens client loyalty.

The quarter in numbers, 17th consecutive revenue increase

The second quarter of 2025 was the 17th consecutive with an increase in management fees and Fee Related Earning, FRE. Management fees increased by 32% year on year, with 87% coming from permanent capital vehicles, something that offers stability to flows.

According to the Company FRE had an increase of 23% and Distributable Earnings +20%.

Capital raising was a record, $12 billion in the quarter and $36 billion in the 12 months, an increase of almost 90% from last year.

In the part of portfolio quality, the annual realized losses in direct lending remained at 13 bps, with no signs of deterioration.

For me, this is critical, it shows that growth is not accompanied by a loosening of credit criteria.

Digital Infrastructure, Real Assets and new capital from EMEA and Asia

The completion of the third flagship fund at $7 billion, with more than half already committed, brings OWL to the forefront of a huge theme, the hyperscale data centers that fuel the growth of AI.

Contracts over 10 years, tenants with an average AA rating, and projects such as the largest data center in the USA combine low-risk and stable flows.

My view? If OWL continues to secure such agreements, this sector can become one of the main drivers of the stock’s valuation in the coming years.

In the real assets sector, OWL raised $5.8 billion in the quarter, also a record. The first phase of OREF VII brought in $2.1 billion, and the net lease segment has grown to three times its size since 2020. The strategy of selling assets with a lower cap rate than the market cap rate produces spread compression and boosts profit. Here OWL shows that it knows how to utilize its position both for income and for capital gains.

The percentage of capital coming from EMEA and APAC investors went up to 23% from 14% in two years. Internationalization is not only for promotion reasons, it gives access to new pools of capital and shields the company against local recessions. For an investor, this means greater resilience of the AUM.

Risks

Despite the steady upward course, Blue Owl Capital is not unaffected by external and internal risks.

The strong dependence on raising new capital means that any slowdown in demand for alternative investments, due to changes in interest rates or geopolitical tensions, can affect the growth rate.

In addition, strategic acquisitions, although so far delivering, carry execution risk, the failure of full integration could reduce synergies and pressure profit margins.

Finally, in the direct lending part, a possible deterioration of credit conditions will increase non-performing loans, limiting the Fee Related Earnings.

However, the stock has given a nice entry point, after the significant correction in the first 4 months of 2025, from 26.5, historical high, to $15. So, considering that in the board the investment concerns have already been absorbed, even after the +30% from the lows, I consider that it remains “cheap”, with large upside potential.

Conclusion

Blue Owl is at a stage where its acquisitions have been fully integrated, the synergies are already producing results and the growth of AUM is moving at levels that few can follow.

The diversification of revenues and the presence in sectors with timeless demand, such as AI driven data centers, alternative credit and net lease real estate, give the company a rare balance of growth and stability.

In my opinion, the market has not yet priced the full scale of this transformation.

If management achieves the target for FRE above $3 billion and management fees above $5 billion, the OWL stock has substantial upside potential over 2 to 3 years.

For investors who want exposure to alternative investments with thematic focus and proven M&A execution capability, OWL is, in my view, an attractive long-term choice, deserving a buy recommendation.

data.title: Hilton Worldwide Holdings: Valuation, Fundamentals, And Technicals Are In Sync
data.description:
Three months after my coverage, Hilton Worldwide Holdings, Inc. (HLT) remains a dominant figure in the hotel industry with its solid performance despite market uncertainty. This is supported by its ideal business model and well-positioned Balance Sheet to ensure its sustainability. With respect to its valuation, overpricing risks are still there. But another key financial metric reveals a possibility of uptrend continuation. Technicals agree with it as momentum remains strong.

HLT Q2 2025: Growth And Profitability Are In Sync

Despite macroeconomic uncertainty, some hotels like Hilton Worldwide Holdings, Inc. remain resilient and unperturbed. This just shows how strong brand recognition, flexible pricing, and wide market presence can propel its topline performance.

Its operating revenue in Q2 2025 reached $3.14B, up by 6.2% YoY from $3.0B. As a mature company, having revenue growth of above 5% can be considered impressive. Not to mention the stubborn inflation and the strained discretionary spending. Its high concentration on its fee-based business model remained the core revenue growth driver of the company. As we can see, all fees from its franchised and managed hotels rose by 7.8% YoY. It was partially offset by the weaker performance in owned and leased properties as the revenue decreased by -1.5% YoY. But overall, it performed well.

On the other hand, its operating costs and expenses increased by 6.0%. This means that revenue increased faster than expenses. It also shows improved efficiency amid its increased operating capacity. As a result, it became more profitable with the operating margin increasing from 24.4% to 24.8% YoY.

Challenges And Laggards

Macroeconomic uncertainty is still a primary challenge for many hotels. Inflation remains stubborn as it stays quite high at 2.7%. This means that the general price level is still increasing today despite the rate cut pause. With that in mind, it’s not surprising that travel perceptions have shifted, unlike in my previous analysis. In a recent study, over half of Americans have cut their summer travel plans due to economic woes and inflation. Some even plan to postpone them. In connection with this, Americans are now geared toward domestic travel via road trips and RVs to fit into their travel spending.

New tariffs are also a challenge due to their potential impact on prices. We should also consider the retaliation of other countries, which can extend to domestic and international travel. Note that these new tariffs have been implemented in many countries two weeks ago. In turn, Americans may become more watchful of their travel costs. As a result, their summer travel budget today is only $3,132, down by -25% from $4,199 YoY.

Why HLT Is Still Solid

One of the things I like about HLT is its high reliance on the fee-based business model. I’ve already said it before, but I would like to highlight it more now. There are 8,807 properties under its name. Yet, it only owns 46 or 0.5% of them. As a result, its actual operating costs and expenses mainly come from these owned properties. In addition, the day-to-day costs and expenses of the remaining 7,761 properties are covered by the actual owners. HLT receives revenues in terms of fees for managing and franchising these properties. It also receives cost reimbursement revenues as payments for covering expenses on behalf of the owners.

We can note that cost reimbursement expenses offset cost reimbursement revenues considering the net value of -$84M ($1,811M - $1,895M). Yet, all the fees it receives are already more than enough to cover these. If we add all the fees and cost reimbursement revenues, the total will be $2,728M or $2.73B ($745M + $97M + $75M + $1,811M), which is 7.3% higher YoY. After subtracting reimbursed expenses, the net value will be $833M ($2,728M - $1,895 M) versus $799M ($2,542M - $1,744M). This means that the fee-based business model is still more profitable than owning and leasing hotels.

Its wide geographical diversification is another core strength. It offers HLT some flexibility in its pricing strategy and some insulation from macroeconomic headwinds. We can see it in the way it adjusts its average daily rates to balance them with occupancy. If we look at the Middle East and Africa, it reduced its ADR by -0.9% to $189.12 to raise occupancy by 7.2% to 70.8%. The thing is, Hilton knows which location has the strongest and weakest demand. That way, it can adjust prices to a desirable level and get optimal results. We can see it in the Americas. Its ADR increased by 4.5%, while occupancy decreased by -0.5%. Yet, the RevPAR increased by 3.8% YoY to $105.81. The same goes for the Asia Pacific as RevPAR increased to $69.21 despite the opposite trend in ADR and occupancy. Lastly, Europe appears to be its strongest segment considering its 77.2% occupancy. This motivated HLT to raise ADR a bit to stabilize occupancy while increasing RevPAR.

Another thing I like about HLT is its robust Balance Sheet. With its asset-light business model, capital requirements are manageable. We can see that its operating cash flow remained enough to cover capex and other investing activities. In fact, it generated an FCF of $635M. This means that it converted 20% of its revenue into free cash using the FCF/Sales Ratio. Its cash level has dropped substantially from Q4 2024 to Q2 2025. Yet, it was mainly due to increased share repurchases and debt repayments.

HLT complements its robust cash flow with prudent debt management to ensure liquidity. At this point, its Net Debt/EBITDA remains decent at 4.0x. This means that its cash and earnings can cover all repayments in four years at once, even if it doesn’t have to. It is also worth noting that HLT has a long debt maturity profile since 45%, or $5.1B of the total amount, has a maturity of at least five years. Hence, HLT has enough capital buffer and time to prepare for repayments.

Valuation

HLT keeps increasing despite the overpricing I noticed in my previous articles. Using its P/S Ratio, HLT trades at 13.55x its sales per share or SPS versus the five-year average of 12.01x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $239.51. It is also worth noting that the price has increased along with SPS over the years. This shows that the market does not really care that much whether the stock price is expensive or not relative to SPS and in previous years. After all, price ratios do not really reveal the intrinsic value of a stock. Instead, they just compare whether the stock is cheaper or costlier than in previous years. What the market really cares about is the actual trend of the SPS. As long as the SPS increases, the stock will also increase.

I used a different metric to look into it more precisely. While the process remains the same, I think comparing the price relative to free cash flow is more realistic since FCF hints at both profitability and sustainability. What's more interesting is that the stock appears cheaper than in previous years. It only trades at 30.48x its FCF versus the average of 35.31. This suggests a TP of $313.16. Hence, the stock may still increase relative to this metric.

Technicals

HLT continues to demonstrate bullish signals, which is consistent with our P/FCF valuation. As we can see, the price continues to create new highs and lows. It also stays above the 50-day and 200-day SMA, showing that trading remains bullish relative to the average in the past 50 and 200 days. In addition, the 50-day SMA is still increasing. This shows that market sentiments are still generally optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and its histogram are still below positive territory. Yet, we can see they are moving upward again. This is consistent with the recent dip when selling volume exceeded buying volume. After all, the stock became overbought, as shown by its RSI. But now, momentum is picking up again as bulls come back. This means that many buyers saw the recent pullback or dip as a good entry point. Lastly, its RSI is now below the overbought region. Hence, there is now enough room for buying.

Conclusion

Hilton Worldwide Holdings, Inc. remains a durable contender in the hotel industry. Although it's still smaller than Marriott (MAR), it shows more robust growth and more stable margins. Liquidity remains decent with its strong operating cash flow and prudent debt management. In addition, technicals still show bullish signals and agree with the P/FCF TP. I am upgrading my rating on this stock from hold to buy.

data.title: Axon Enterprise: Current Valuation Signals Caution, Reasons To Hold
data.description:
When I last covered Axon Enterprise (AXON) on March 13, 2025, I labeled it as "a buy for aggressive growth investors comfortable with high-growth companies" that pursue moonshot opportunities. Investors who chose to follow that advice saw their investment rise 37.54% compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 15.22% rise, as of the August 12, 2025 market close. Solid first and second quarter earnings supported the stock's rise.

The stock rose 14.12% on May 8, in reaction to the company releasing its first-quarter results showing revenue growth of 31% year-over-year, driven by strong demand for its premium software offerings, TASER 10, Axon Body 4, and platform solutions. The strength of the company's first quarter led management to raise its full-year revenue and adjusted EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) guidance.

Axon followed that up with excellent second-quarter 2025 results on August 4, 2025, where management once again raised full-year 2025 revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance, citing strong performance and confidence in its pipeline. The stock rose 16.41% on August 5 in reaction to robust earnings. Despite the excellent earnings, there are reasons some investors may back away from buying the stock.

Some considered Axon overvalued when I recommended it in March. The stock's appreciation over the last five months has now put its valuation into the stratosphere. Seeking Alpha's quant rates Axon's valuation as an F. At its current valuation, I consider the company a high-risk investment. Although the company has increased its recurring revenue from subscriptions, the company's Platforms Solutions segment (which includes counter-drone technology) has large international or enterprise contracts that can introduce volatility into the timing of revenue recognition. This revenue lumpiness may cause the company to miss future revenue estimates, which may displease analysts and investors, who may punish the stock, especially with the high expectations built into the stock price.

Although the company has a long potential growth runway, people choosing to invest in it at the current valuation may not see much upside from it in the short term and may even see some short-term downside. The long-term potential upside may justify existing investors continuing to hold. However, those who currently don't own shares may want to avoid it at the current valuation.

Reasons growth investors are interested in Axon Enterprise

Axon Enterprise started as TASER International in 1993, initially selling Tasers (electroshock weapons), and later expanding into body cameras. If its business remained at the level of making one-time sales of hardware, investors wouldn't be nearly as interested in this company. However, Axon has evolved into more than just a body camera and Taser company.

The company entered the Software as a Service ("SaaS") business in 2008 when it launched Axon Evidence (formerly Evidence.com). The company later expanded its cloud business beyond Axon Evidence to include services such as software solutions for computer-assisted dispatch, fleet management, accounting, and other productivity software. Axon also integrated its software into its hardware. Today, when customers buy its body cams, Tasers, and other hardware, they also purchase subscriptions to its software.

Subscription businesses, unlike one-time sales, generate consistent, predictable, recurring revenue. Investors will often award companies producing steady revenue from subscription-based businesses a higher valuation. When we get to the valuation section of this article, remember that one reason the market awarded Axon a premium valuation is that it has a healthy and growing subscription business.

Although acquiring new customers is essential for its subscription business's initial growth, the market measures the long-term health and profitability of a subscription business by its ability to grow revenue from its existing customer base. The best way for Axon to increase revenue from its existing customer base is to create a range of products to upsell and cross-sell to that customer base. Selling products to existing customers has a significantly lower Customer Acquisition Cost ("CAC") than making sales to new customers. Upselling and cross-selling also increase a customer's Lifetime Value ("LTV"). When a company increases its LTV and lowers its CAC, it begins generating more revenue from each customer than it spends to acquire them, a prelude to achieving long-term profitability.

Axon is aggressively introducing new products to upsell and cross-sell. Chief Executive Officer Patrick W. Smith said on the second-quarter 2025 earnings call:

First, demand for new technology from our customers is accelerating, and it's outpacing even my most optimistic expectations. Artificial intelligence, drones and robotics, real-time operations, cameras and our newest TASER devices and virtual reality, each of those are resonating across our customer base. There's no one breakout product driving conversations. It's everything. In the past, it could take a few years for our newest products to start seeing meaningful adoption. Often at least the first year took it to fine-tune the application, work through the approval processes and get everything right. Today, we're watching customers adopt new solutions as a standard faster and in real time.

Axon is quickly transitioning from a company known for only a few key hardware products (TASERs and body cameras) to a much broader interconnected product line within its public safety technology ecosystem. One reason the company has achieved a premium valuation is that the market has noticed the increased upselling and cross-selling of new products, which bodes well for the company's long-term profitability.

The company continues rolling out new cutting-edge products that customers are eager to snap up. Axon is among the first to bring artificial intelligence ("AI") to the public safety market. In late 2024, the company rolled out its AI era plan, a subscription to its suite of artificial intelligence productivity tools.

The above image shows one of its newest subscription plans, the Officer Safety Plan 10 Premium with the AI Era Plan. In the first year of this plan, most of the revenue recognized comes from hardware. In the following years, most of the revenue recognized comes from subscriptions to Software and Services, as well as AI Era products. This plan should generate a significant amount of annual recurring software revenue (75%+ software revenue over a 5-year term).

Another reason that it has a premium valuation is that it is a rapidly growing market leader in an attractive market.

Although Axon is one of the most well-known companies in the public safety and security technology space, it only generated trailing 12-month ("TTM") revenue of $2.392 billion at the end of the second quarter, or 1.85% of its total addressable market of $129 billion. So, it still has a long revenue growth runway.

The market believes the company's second-quarter financial results and guidance support the company's premium valuation.

Axon Enterprise's second-quarter 2025 financial results

Axon's second quarter 2025 revenue increased 33% year over year, its 14th straight quarter exceeding 25% revenue growth. Its second-quarter 2025 revenue was $669 million, beating analysts' estimates by $27.57 million. The company's second-quarter 2025 shareholder letter stated revenue was "driven by strong premium software adoption and robust demand for TASER 10, Axon Body 4 and counter-drone equipment."

Its second-quarter Connected Devices revenue increased 29% year-over-year to $376 million. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Brittany Bagley stated on the company's second quarter 2025 earnings call, "This growth was driven by strength across categories, including TASER, which grew 19%, driven by TASER 10. Personal sensors grew 24%, driven by Axon Body 4."

Axon's Software & Services increased 39% year over year to $292 million.

Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Brittany Bagley stated on the company's second quarter 2025 earnings call:

In terms of translating that into revenue today, the top line growth continues to be driven by software and services. Which grew 39% year-over-year to $292 million. Our ability to win new users and to drive adoption of our newest products underpins this continued growth. Net revenue retention [NRR] increased to 124% and has been near or above 120% for 20 consecutive quarters.

An NRR above 120% means that existing customers spent 20% more money over the previous year due to customers upgrading to higher-priced plans, expanding their usage of a product or service, or purchasing additional products or services, minus customer churn or downgrading to lower-priced plans or reducing their usage. The market considers an NRR above 120% as a sign of customer loyalty and a healthy subscription-based business model.

The company's Software & Services adjusted gross margin was 79%. Its Connected Devices adjusted gross margin was 51%. As Software & Services grow faster than Connected Devices, the company's total adjusted gross margin should improve.

Axon's adjusted gross margin increased 20 basis points ("bps") year-over-year to 63.3%. CFO Bagley stated on the earnings call that gross margin was "driven by product mix to software and services, partially offset by lower devices margin due to the strong growth in our newer hardware products and newer markets."

The following table shows Axon's GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) to non-GAAP reconciliation. Its non-GAAP gross margin excludes stock-based compensation ("SBC"), amortization of acquired intangible assets, inventory step-up amortization, and payroll taxes related to 2024 employee XSP vesting. The company's second quarter 2025 SBC as a percentage of quarterly revenue is 20.82%, a level that some investors may have an issue with because of the potential stock dilution.

The following shows Axon's reconciliation from EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) to adjusted EBITDA.

The company's second quarter 2025 adjusted EBITDA increased 70 bps year over year to 25.7%. CFO Bagley said on the earnings call that adjusted EBITDA "came in ahead of expectations due to higher revenue and operating leverage as well as benefiting from the timing of tariffs, which will now impact us more in the second half of the year."

The company's second-quarter GAAP earnings per share ("EPS") are $0.44, beating analysts' estimates by $0.46.

Its second-quarter TTM cash from operations is $275.6 million. Axon's second-quarter TTM capital expenditure ("CapEx") was $99.09 million. The company's second-quarter TTM free cash flow ("FCF") was $176.49 million.

The company's cash and short-term investments at the end of the second quarter 2025 were $2.243 billion. Its long-term debt was $2.051 billion.

The company's debt-to-equity ratio is 0.75, meaning the company finances its operations with equity more than debt. Its debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 73.69. The market generally considers a debt-to-EBITDA ratio higher than four as a sign that a company may face significant challenges in repaying its debt obligations.

As a result, Moody's and S&P rated Axon's bond issuance in March with Ba3/BB+ ratings, with positive and stable outlooks, respectively. Ba3/BB+ ratings are non-investment grade speculative, AKA junk bonds. Axon's current ratio of 2.95 means the company can pay its short-term obligations.

The company raised its full-year 2025 guidance to between $2.65 billion and $2.73 billion. Before the company released its updated 2025 revenue guidance, analysts estimated full-year 2025 revenue guidance of $2.66 billion, slightly below management's forecast of $2.69 billion at the midpoint. Following the company's release of updated guidance, analysts raised their 2025 revenue estimates to $2.71 billion. CFO Bagley stated on the earnings call, "This [guidance] reflects our performance in Q2 and our confidence in the pipeline for the second half."

Axon raised its adjusted EBITDA guidance to between $665 million and $685 million. If it hits the midpoint of the company guidance, adjusted EBITDA will grow 29.55% year over year. If the company reaches the midpoint of management's revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance, its adjusted EBITDA margin will remain flat at 25%. CFO Bagley said on the earnings call:

This [guidance] maintains our 25% margin target for the year and incorporates our planned investments and tariff-related expenses in the second half. We continue to expect to increase hiring over the remainder of the year, particularly in R&D, as we prioritize investing behind the incredible product road map we've talked about as well as in our exciting new markets.

Before management's updated adjusted EBITDA guidance, analysts estimated 2025 EPS of $6.19. Following the company's release of its updated adjusted EBITDA guidance, analysts raised their 2025 EPS estimates to $6.66.

Axon raised its CapEx guidance to between $170 million - $185 million. The company uses CapEx to expand its capacity to manufacture its products, such as the TASER 10 and Axon Body 4 cameras. CFO Bagley stated on the earnings call:

We are continuing to ramp TASER 10 capacity. I would say we are sort of continuing to ramp TASER 10 capacity as much as we can because we continue to see just great demand for that. So we're ramping it this year. You saw us take our CapEx guidance up a bit. That's us continuing to ramp it into next year so that we continue to meet that demand.

The company's increase in CapEx presents a headwind to FCF growth in 2025. Growth investors who understand that Axon needs to invest heavily to take advantage of its global opportunities are more interested in revenue growth rather than FCF growth at this stage of the company's maturity.

Valuation

Axon Enterprise's August 12, 2025 price to sales (P/S) is 25.93, up from its P/S ratio of 19.84 in my March article, and exceeding its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year median, suggesting overvaluation. If it sold at its three-year median of 12.98, its price would be $365.90, a 52.39% decline from its August 12 market close of $768.57.

The following table compares Axon Enterprise's forward P/S ratio, P/S/G ratio, and profitability margins to companies that sell self-defense, body cam, or drone products.

Company Name One-year forward P/S ratio one-year forward estimated revenue growth one-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) TTM FCF margin TTM operating margin TTM Gross margin TTM Return on invested capital (ROIC)
Axon Enterprise 23.69 30.30% 0.78 7.38% 9.22% 60.47% -0.06
Byrna Technologies (BYRN) 4.19 35.31% 0.12 -8.34% 9.60% 61.88% 28.31%

L3Harris Technologies (LHX) 2.34 1.26% 1.86 10.12% 11.07% 25.74% 5.35%
Textron (TXT) 0.93 8.34% 0.11 3.98% 5.89% 19.46% 7.56%
SoundThinking (SSTI) 1.27 8.93% 0.14 11.33% -6.35 56.77% -9.79%
Red Cat Holdings (RCAT) 11.50 349.29% 0.03 -255% -278.3% -1.25% -126.3%
AeroVironment (AVAV) 6.08 144.34% 0.04 -2.94 38.83% 7.21% 4.97%

Some investors would consider Axon Enterprise overvalued based on the above metrics in this relative valuation comparison.

Axon's current 2025 forward P/S ratio is 23.69, which is much higher than all other companies listed, indicating that investors are willing to pay a premium price per dollar of sales for Axon compared to its peers.

A P/S/G ratio measures a company's P/S ratio relative to its growth. A low P/S/G ratio indicates the market may undervalue the stock, suggesting investors are getting more growth for a relatively low price. Conversely, a high P/S/G ratio can indicate the market may overvalue the stock, as the market may be paying a premium for its growth.

Axon's P/S/G ratio is 0.78. On a relative valuation basis, Axon sells at a premium P/S/G to all its peers, except Motorola and L3Harris Technologies, which may deserve a premium over all the companies in the comparison due to those companies' positive operating margin, FCF margin, and ROIC.

However, it's questionable whether Axon's profitability, FCF margins, and ROIC are high enough to justify a premium valuation over Byrna Technologies, Textron, SoundThinking, or AeroVironment. For instance, Axon has a negative ROIC of -0.06, meaning it's not generating a return on its invested capital, which some investors consider a bad sign.

Although several of the companies on the list have either negative FCF or operating margins, some may think that Axon's FCF margins and operating margins are not high enough to justify a substantially higher P/S/G.

Axon Enterprise's TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 190.09, well above the industrials sector median of 25.00, suggesting overvaluation.

Axon's 2025 forward PEG ratio is 9.46, calculated by dividing its 2025 forward P/E of 115.34 by its EPS growth rate of 12.18%. The market's general rule of thumb considers a PEG ratio above 2.0 as overvalued. As a result, some investors would consider Axon as grossly overvalued. If it sold at a PEG ratio of 2.0, the stock price would be $162.23, a 78.89% decline from its August 12 closing price.

Axon's price to FCF (P/FCF) is 351.34, substantially higher than its three-, five-, and seven-year medians, a sign of overvaluation. If it sold at its three-year median, its stock price would be $318.35, a 58.57% decline from its August 12 market close.

However, a high P/FCF compared to its median could also mean that the market has high expectations for Axon's FCF growth. Analysts currently expect its FCF growth to meaningfully accelerate from 12.67% in 2025 to 75.35% in 2026 and 41.8% in 2027.

Axon's FCF yield is 0.30%. A general rule of thumb when assessing FCF yields is that when a stock trades at the higher end of its FCF yield trading range, the market undervalues the stock, and when it trades at the lower end of its range, the market overvalues the stock. According to that rule, the market currently is paying a higher price for a smaller amount of free cash flow, potentially overvaluing Axon's FCF. However, the company is still in the relatively early stages of its growth story, and growth investors may not use FCF to value it at the company's current maturity level.

Risks

Axon issued $1.75 billion in senior notes in March to support its growth initiatives and retire convertible notes. The new debt's higher interest rate of 6.125% notes due in 2030 and $750 million of 6.250% notes due in 2033 will increase the company's interest expense, potentially producing headwinds to its profitability, especially if revenue growth slows down. Additionally, as I stated earlier, the company has an unfavorable debt-to-EBITDA ratio. If the company's growth initiatives fail to generate a sufficient return on investment, the debt could become a significant burden.

The company has significant execution risk. Large international and enterprise contracts are an essential driver of revenue growth. Suppose management fails to close large deals promptly and, more importantly, fails to deliver the promised services; bookings and revenue growth would decline. Additionally, the inability to execute and deliver promised services would damage the company's reputation with customers, sour investor sentiment, result in analysts' downgrades, harm the core investment thesis of increasing sales to existing customers, and likely result in a substantial sell-off.

Axon Management makes significant bets on cutting-edge technology such as AI Era Plan, drones, anti-drone technology, and TASER 10. The company's high revenue growth is highly dependent on the rapid adoption of these technologies. Some of these products have substantial regulatory and legal risks that may impede the rapid adoption of these technologies. For instance, within the U.S., some states have laws prohibiting the use of drones for random surveillance or with facial recognition capabilities. Additionally, federal laws such as the Aircraft Sabotage Act and the Aircraft Piracy Act prohibit using counter-drone measures near airports or in populated areas, and only a few federal agencies are authorized to use those measures. The company's AI Era Plan contains some products that have potential civil liberty concerns regarding data privacy, surveillance, and constitutional rights. As a result, investors should be aware that various privacy groups and the public in general could push back on some of its AI initiatives and stall revenue growth in some AI products. Anything that slows or prevents Axon from upselling or cross-selling additional products hurts the company's investing thesis.

Downgrading Axon to Hold

Over the long term, there is a lot to like about this company. Axon's long-term potential revenue growth runway and its transition to a subscription business, strengthening its long-term profitability, are significant reasons that the market remains interested in this stock. Additionally, the public safety market is resistant to a broad economic slowdown.

However, the company's potentially volatile revenue recognition, combined with a very high valuation, increases the risk of a revenue miss causing a significant sell-off in the short term. Value investors or people with an investing time horizon of less than a year should avoid investing in Axon at the current valuation. Even long-term investors with an interest in the stock likely can find better entry points over the next year.

If you have an existing position, continue to hold. However, if you have yet to purchase the stock, wait for a better valuation before making a purchase.

data.title: SHLD: The Defense ETF That's Crushing Big Tech
data.description:
While I remain on largely bullish on big tech the frothy valuations of some of the companies concern me. This is why one SHLD has caught my attention due to it both crushing indices like QQQ over the past three years while also having exposure to the defense industry, one which has a robust growth profile. Since researching SHLD I have since bought shares and have concluded that it deserves a Strong Buy rating.

This article will analyze SHLD against its peers and explain why it's in such a strong position moving forward.

Overview

The Global X Defense Tech ETF (NYSEARCA:SHLD) aims to mirror the performance and the yield of the Global X Defense Tech Index. SHLD invests in companies that are considered leaders in cyber security, AI, hardware, and military systems. The ETF was incepted in September 2023 and has $3.5 billion of assets under management with an expense ratio of 0.50%.

The ETF has high concentration risk as the top ten holdings comprises 62% of its total value, and presently only has 45 holdings.

Some of the largest holdings include Palantir, RTX Corporation, Rheinmetall AG, and Lockheed Martin.

SHLD is passively managed, meaning that the manager does not try to beat the index nor make any short-term technical plays or shifts. It's a physical replication model meaning that it purchases actual shares to replicate the index.

For a company to be included in the index, more than 50% of its revenue must come from defense-tech and adjacent industries. This includes industries such as cybersecurity, Internet of Things (IOT), and advanced military systems.

The index is free-float and market-cap weighted. This means that a company cannot comprise more than 8% of the index's total value. There are also volume, regional, and liquidity requirements that firms must meet in order to be included.

Valuation

SHLD's value has increased 153% over the past three years, and grew 68.12% year-to-date alone. Investors might worry that its rally might be overheated, but analyst forecasts for the top nine of its holdings show promising median revenue, EPS, and share price increases.

For the top nine holdings, the group is expected to grow its EPS by an average of 12.59%, while revenue is expected to grow 5.27%. PLTR is the notable outlier among its peers. Although its absolute weighting is capped at 8%, it's driving a significant percentage of the average revenue and EPS increases for the group. If one already feels bullish on PLTR, then the bull case becomes stronger for SHLD and vice-versa.

In sum, with accretive revenue, EPS, and share price increases already forecasted for SHLD, it lays the groundwork for a continued rally.

Comparing SHLD to its peers paints an even clearer picture that it's in a strong position to continue to surge higher. On a total return basis, SHLD returned 156%, far exceeding the returns of my constructed peer group that includes ITA, XAR, PPA, and NATO.

SHLD has a 1-year expense-adjusted return of 71.6% which is far ahead of its peer group. The reason for its core outperformance comes from a sharper tilt toward defense-tech names such as Palantir. Liquidity is robust at $67.8M daily turnover and its expense ratio of 0.50% is the highest among its peers, but still competitive for a thematic ETF.
